claim.hypotheses_location: Pages 752–753: Hypotheses H1 and H2 stated under the general model (Fig. 1); H3 and H4 stated at the start of Section 2 (“Influence of the basic components on the development of leaders via experience”).
claim.statement_location: Abstract (page 749): authors propose a developmental model linking secure attachment (lower trait anxiety, higher openness) to leadership development via experiences.

data.source: Self-report questionnaires administered to Israel Defense Forces (IDF) soldiers during basic training.
data.wave_or_subset: Four companies: two armored (n=169) and two infantry (n=117). Excluded 10–20% new immigrants with language difficulties; additional absences for technical reasons.
data.sample_size: 286 male soldiers (analysis samples varied due to missing data; SEM models report N≈236–240).
data.notes: Participants were male IDF conscripts near the end of basic training; anonymity assured; response rates ~65% (armored) and ~55% (infantry). Measures included adult attachment (anxiety and avoidance dimensions; secure computed as low anxiety/avoidance), Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI-T), a 24-item Openness to Experiences scale adapted from NEO-PI-R, and a 7-item Leadership-Shaping Experiences index. Parceling used to create three indicators per latent construct. Pilot study on 195 recruits preceded main data collection. Final SEM analyses used listwise cases yielding N≈236–240.

method.description: Cross-sectional survey of IDF soldiers assessing attachment style, trait anxiety, openness to experience, and recalled leadership-shaping experiences from childhood to enlistment. The theoretical model posited that secure attachment predicts lower trait anxiety and higher openness, which in turn predict greater leadership-related experiences. Structural equation modeling (SEM) tested the hypothesized and alternative causal models.
steps: Recruit IDF soldiers from two armored and two infantry companies near the end of basic training; obtain consent and assure anonymity.

Administer self-report questionnaires: adult attachment (anxiety and avoidance dimensions), Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI-T), 24-item Openness to Experiences scale, and 7-item Leadership-Shaping Experiences (LSE) questionnaire.
Score instruments; compute secure attachment as low anxiety and low avoidance.
Create item parcels (randomly) to form three indicators per latent variable.
Estimate measurement model (CFA) to assess factor structure and validity.
Estimate structural model (SEM) with maximum likelihood in AMOS 5 for the hypothesized causal paths.
Test alternative structural models (including direct paths from attachment to leadership experiences and models with different independent variables).
Evaluate model fit (χ2, CFI, NFI, NNFI/TLI, GFI, RMSEA) and interpret standardized path coefficients and explained variance.
method.models: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) with maximum likelihood estimation; measurement model via CFA with parceling; tested six structural models including the hypothesized model (attachment → trait anxiety and openness → leadership experiences) and alternatives with direct paths and different causal orderings.
method.outcome_variable: Leadership experiences (Leadership-Shaping Experiences index; 7 Likert items on responsibility, roles, and influence at home, school, and social settings).
method.independent_variables: Secure attachment style (low anxiety and low avoidance), trait anxiety (STAI-T), openness to experiences (24-item OE scale).
method.tools_software: AMOS 5 for SEM; parceling procedures applied to all multi-item constructs.

results.summary: The hypothesized SEM model fit the data well. Secure attachment was strongly and negatively related to trait anxiety and positively related to openness. Trait anxiety negatively predicted leadership experiences, while openness positively predicted leadership experiences. The model explained 19% of the variance in leadership experiences. A direct path from secure attachment to leadership experiences was not significant. Several alternative models were tested; model fit and path significance favored the hypothesized structure or a variant allowing correlation between attachment and anxiety.

metadata.original_paper_title: Attachment and leader's development via experiences
